cards.text: An identifier's {{c1::scope}} determines where the identifier can be {{c2::accessed}} within the source code.
{{c1::Local scope}} refers to variables defined inside a {{c2::function}} or {{c3::block}}. They can only be accessed within that function/block.
Variables defined inside a function are called {{c1::local variables}}. They are created when the function is {{c2::called}} and destroyed when it {{c3::returns}}.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
void doSomething() {
int x = 5;
}

int main() {
std::cout << x; // Error!
return 0;
}
cards.answer: Error: x is not in scope. x is a local variable in doSomething() and cannot be accessed from main().
cards.explanation: Local variables are only accessible within the function where they're defined. x exists only inside doSomething().

cards.text: Function {{c1::parameters}} have {{c2::local scope}} - they can only be accessed within the {{c3::function body}}, just like local variables.

cards.question: Can two functions have local variables with the same name?
cards.code:
void funcA() {
int x = 5;
}

void funcB() {
int x = 10; // OK?
}
cards.answer: Yes, this is fine. Each x has local scope limited to its own function, so they don't conflict.
cards.explanation: Local variables in different functions are independent, even if they have the same name.

cards.text: The region of code where a variable can be accessed is called its {{c1::scope}}. Variables go out of scope when they reach the end of the {{c2::block}} in which they were defined.
Local variables are {{c1::created}} at the point of definition and {{c2::destroyed}} at the end of the block (typically when the function {{c3::returns}}).

cards.explanation:
Local scope provides important advantages:
  • Variables don't interfere with each other across functions
  • Easier to reason about code - limited area to check
  • Prevents accidental modification from other code
  • Automatic cleanup when function ends
examples: void calcA() { int result = 5; }  // result only exists here
void calcB() { int result = 10; }  // Different result, no conflict

cards.question: What is the scope of x in this code?
cards.code:
int main() {
int x = 5;
{
int y = 10;
} // y destroyed here
// x still exists
return 0;
}
cards.answer: x has scope for the entire main() function. y has scope only within the inner block.
cards.explanation: Variables defined in inner blocks have narrower scope than variables in outer blocks.

cards.text: A block is defined by curly braces {{c1::{ }}}. Variables defined in a block go out of scope at the {{c2::closing brace}}.

cards.front: Can a local variable in an outer block access a variable in an inner nested block?
cards.back: No. Variables in inner blocks are not accessible to outer blocks. Scope only works outward, not inward.
